title: X-Leg Desk with Drawer
summary: A campaign-style desk on crossed X legs, built from laminated 1x3 layers so each leg reads as one solid angled piece with no visible joints.
description:
Each leg on this desk is built from multiple 1x3 pieces glued and nailed face to face rather than cut from one thick blank — the trick is lining every edge up flush as you laminate, which is what lets the finished leg read as a single solid piece and saves real sanding work later. Every leg piece is cut at compound angles (20° and 35° depending on which end), so lay out a full dry run before cutting anything for real; a dry run is what catches a mismatched angle while it's still just an arrangement of loose parts.

The two leg X-assemblies need to end up with an identical 23-1/8" spread at the bottom — check that measurement before the glue sets on each one, not after. The drawer, if you're building one, gets sized to the actual desk opening (1" narrower than the opening) rather than to a fixed number, and sits slightly inset when closed, which is the look this style is going for rather than a flaw to fix.

tools.note: The compound 20°/35° angle cuts on every leg piece need an accurate miter saw.

materials.name: 1x3 board, 8 ft (sharp, square edges)
materials.note: Shop for boards with genuinely sharp, square edges — the laminated legs depend on tight glue lines.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 35° off square, parallel to each other.
Both ends cut at 35° off square, parallel to each other.
One end at 20° off square, other end at 35°, measured to the longest points.
One end at 20°, other at 35°, longest-point measurement.
One end at 20°, other at 35°. Dry-fit against your assembled legs to confirm orientation before final cuts.
Front apron is optional — skip it if building the drawer version.
Two layers stacked per side for extra drawer depth.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: From the 1x3, cut two long leg pieces at 35-3/4" and two shorter full leg pieces at 31-3/8", all four with both ends at 35 degrees off square and parallel to each other; four leg bottom fillers at 17-1/2", two leg top fillers at 14" and two notch supports at 18-1/4", each of those eight angled filler/support pieces with one end at 20 degrees and the other at 35 degrees. Also from the 1x3 cut the 45" bottom stretcher, four drawer sides at 15-1/4", two drawer front/back at 12" and the 44" drawer face. From the 12 ft 1x4 cut two side aprons at 24" with both ends at 35 degrees, and two aprons at 45"; the front one is optional and gets skipped if you are building the drawer version. Every angled length above is a longest-point measurement, so mark the long point and label every piece as it comes off the saw. The desktop panel is bought at 24" x 48" and needs no cutting; cut the 16" x 44" drawer bottom from the 1/4" plywood or hardboard.

steps.title: Dry-fit the legs
steps.body: Lay out all the leg pieces for both leg sets before gluing anything — a dry run catches a mismatched angle now, while it's cheap to fix.

steps.title: Build the long legs
steps.body: Start with the two longest leg pieces. You're building two mirror-image leg sets, so keep track of which is which. Attach the 17-1/2" leg fillers to the bottoms of the long pieces with 1-1/4" finish nails and wood glue, taking great care to line the edges up flush — that's what creates the illusion of a single solid leg and saves sanding later.

steps.title: Build the short legs
steps.body: Do the same lamination for the shorter full leg pieces. The middle angles are all 20° off square; the top and bottom angles are 35°.

steps.title: Cross and join each leg set
steps.body: Position one short leg and one long leg crossing in the middle to form an X. Check the bottom spread measures exactly 23-1/8" before gluing and nailing down. Build the second leg set as a mirror image.

steps.title: Add the top filler
steps.body: Fit the long top leg piece with its matching short filler piece and nail down.

steps.title: Add the notch supports
steps.body: Position the notch supports into the notch formed where the crossed legs meet, and glue and nail down.

steps.title: Attach the side and back aprons
steps.body: The side aprons sit to the inside of the leg assemblies. Attach the back apron as positioned in your reference. For a drawerless desk, add the front apron the same way now.

steps.title: Add the bottom stretcher
steps.body: The bottom stretcher is a 1x3, attached the same way as the back apron.

steps.title: Attach the desktop
steps.body: If you have a pocket-hole jig, drill 3/4" pocket holes in the aprons before you attach the side and back aprons, then attach the desktop through them with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. Otherwise, nail and glue the desktop down with 2" finish nails.

steps.title: Build and install the drawer
steps.body:
Build the drawer box from the sides, front, and back, and attach the plywood or hardboard bottom with 1-1/4" finish nails and wood glue. Check the drawer is square and overall 1" narrower than the desk's drawer opening.

Attach the drawer slide members to the bottom side corners of the drawer box, and the matching cabinet members to the inside of the desk, following your drawer slide manufacturer's instructions. When closed, the drawer should sit slightly inset on the desk front — that's the intended look, not a fit problem to chase.

images.alt: Campaign-style desk with crossed X legs and an inset drawer
